responseSet.title: Sublime subjects: aesthetic experience and intersubjectivity in psychoanalysis: by Giuseppe Civitarese, London, Routledge, 2017, £37.99, ISBN: 9781138505254
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sublime subjects: aesthetic experience and intersubjectivity in psychoanalysis

by Giuseppe Civitarese, London, Routledge, 2017, £37.99, ISBN: 9781138505254

Ruggero Levy

Ruggero Levy

Ruggero Levy   ruggerolevy@gmail.com

I would like to begin the review of Giuseppe Civitarese's book by highlighting its importance. I believe that it is, in fact, a milestone within psychoanalytic publications because, in a consistent way, it contributes to the foundation of a new paradigm within psychoanalysis, which originates in the contributions of Wilfred Bion. This is a paradigm belonging to the intersubjective psychoanalytic model; however, it places the aesthetic experience of the analytic pair at the centre of the psychoanalytic connection, the development of a sense of truth, and mental growth. Furthermore, Civitarese goes beyond simply studying aesthetic experience by placing it in the field of the aesthetic of the sublime, a current of thought established in the eighteenth century. The author of Sublime Subjects, in an erudite way, digs up evidence of the influence of the pre-Romantic and Romantic sublime aesthetic in Bion's concepts throughout his work. For that purpose, he studies fundamental concepts in the work of Bion, identifying their roots’ aesthetic conceptions originating in the eighteenth century, which had a profound influence on poetry, literature, fine arts and philosophy. Bion's basic conceptions – of O, the darkness, the distance and the union, the reverie, the passion and the language of achievement, the genius and the mystic, the aesthetic conflict, the stupor, the transformations, the catastrophic change and becoming infinite – are all traced to their origins in the aesthetic of sublime.

Meltzer (1984), in his book Dream Life, studies the advances in psychoanalytic metapsychology accomplished by Klein and Bion. He states that, in addition to the four classic metapsychological dimensions of psychoanalysis established by Freud – the genetic, the dynamic, the topographic and the economic – Melanie Klein introduced the geographical dimension and Bion the epistemological dimension. According to Meltzer, following the contributions of the two English authors, when analysing a psychic event, in addition to the classic metapsychological dimensions mentioned above, we should also understand the geography of the phenomenon (whether it is situated in the interior world of the subject or has been projected onto an object), as well as understanding the phenomenon from the point of view of knowledge (K link) – that is, whether it is at the service of knowing the psychic reality of the subject or of the object, or if it functions as an obstacle to this understanding.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality, excess, and representation

by Rosine Jozef Perelberg, London, Routledge, 2020, 204 pp., £29.99 (paperback), ISBN: 978-0-367-25355-4

Björn Sahlberg

Björn Sahlberg

Björn Sahlberg   bjorn.sahlberg@icloud.com

I would like to designate this ground-breaking work as “Contemporary Freudian”: it is fresh and contemporary, integrating a rich field of various psychoanalytic ideas on firm Freudian ground. Perelberg discusses many of Freud's most important writings on both the topographical and the structural models of the mind. In addition, Perelberg integrates, in a masterful way, important aspects from the three traditions in the British Society—Independent, Contemporary Freudian, and Kleinian—but especially Winnicott, who is an important source of inspiration. Sexuality, Excess, and Representation, like her previous book, Psychic Bisexuality: A French–British Dialogue, is also a truly “British–French dialogue,” where, among others, the voices of Lacan, André Green, Laplanche, the Botellas and Chabert can be heard. It is refreshing that Perelberg does not get bogged down in old controversies, either in Britain or in France. Instead, she takes her inspiration from all the best sources available and, in the process, creates her own unique perspective.

As Perelberg says,

Psychoanalysis has, since Freud, viewed sexuality in itself as traumatic to the individual. This traumatic dimension is linked to the force of the drives in that there is always something in excess that cannot be reduced to the level of representation. (10)

The traumatic dimension of sexuality also relates to the recognition of the difference between the sexes, the result of a long process of development and elaboration. In its homosexual and heterosexual dimensions, the Oedipal configuration confronts the child with the impossibility of belonging to both sexes, as well as the impossibility of possessing their parents. This establishes a link between sexuality, castration, renunciation, taboo and prohibition. (10)

According to Perelberg,

the revolutionary contribution made by Freud is the idea that one is not born already formed as a man or woman, but one is constituted as such in the process of development. (10)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creative states of mind: psychoanalysis and the artist’s process

by Patricia Townsend, Abingdon and New York, Routledge, 2019, 136 pp., £34.99 (pbk), ISBN: 9780367146160, £120.00 (hbk), ISBN: 9780367146146

Steven Groarke

Steven Groarke

Steven Groarke   s.groarke@roehampton.ac.uk

Patricia Townsend's Creative States of Mind is primarily a book about experience. How does one make use of experience? Townsend addresses this question through a fascinating account of her own uncanny experience of Morecambe Bay, the largest expanse of intertidal mudflats and sand in the UK. She was troubled by her encounter with the landscape; something was happening that she could not fathom. The disturbing feelings remained vague and unformed, resonating with associations beyond any conscious awareness or knowledge of the history of the estuary. Stirred by something in the world that she did not know her way around to make a work of art, she set about experimenting with photography and video animation. An impulse to something like a work put her under pressure and, in this case, the reach for meaning involved a deliberate use of various media, but also a less conscious sense of what felt right. The developing artwork was dependent on what worked for Townsend on both levels, conscious and unconscious; it had to be convincing. Eventually, she came up with the idea of animating a series of photographs of the channels of water in the sands, an idea that incorporated the not-yet-conceived aspects of her original experience. The idea was therefore realized in an artwork replete with traces (uncanny effects) of the disturbance at its origin.

Townsend went on to create a series of works that embodied the strangely familiar feeling that the threatening sand and the dangers of the Bay evoked in her; each new work elaborated further on her experience of this particular landscape. What, exactly, is happening here? What was it that caused Townsend to think and wonder in the first place, what was she given to 1283

responseSet.title: Practices of joint meaning creation. Dreams in psychoanalytic discussion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Practices of joint meaning creation. Dreams in psychoanalytic discussion

Anssi Peräkylä and Jörg Bergmann

Jörg Bergmann

Helsinki
Finland

Bielefeld
Germany

Jörg Bergmann  

Dream, meaning creation, conversation analysis, interaction, discourse

Using conversation analysis of audio recorded psychoanalytic sessions, this article investigates dream interpretation as conversational practice. We focus on the ways in which the “real world” meanings of objects or events in the dream are collaboratively created. Three routes for the meaning creation were found. (1) In plain assertions, either the analyst or the patient asserts the meaning of a dream element, for example stating that the cow in the dream means women. (2) In meaning creation through redescription, the analyst describes anew events belonging to the real world or the dream, which have been referred to in the earlier conversation. This redescription makes possible the subsequent assertion of explicit linkages between the dream and the real world. (3) In the merging of referential worlds, the analyst extends the patient’s real-world description with images that are recognisably from the dream: the world of the dream and the real world are thus momentarily merged. In discussion, we point out that in our audio recorded data, the dream interpretation does not primarily involve revealing repressed and unconscious ideas, but rather it involves reminding the patient of something that the patient already knows but is reluctant to think or talk about.

À l’aide de la méthode dite « conversation analysis » de séances psychanalytiques enregistrées, l’article étudie l’interprétation des rêves comme pratique dialogique. Nous nous concentrons sur la façon dont les significations du « monde réel » des objets ou des événements dans le rêve sont créées conjointement. Trois trajectoires pour la création de sens ont été trouvées. (1) Par énonciation simple, l’analyste ou le patient affirme la signification d’un élément de rêve, par exemple en déclarant que la vache dans le rêve signifie « femme ». (2) Par création de sens à travers la redescription, l’analyste décrit de nouveau des événements appartenant au monde réel ou à celle du rêve, mentionnés précédemment. Cette nouvelle description rend possible l’affirmation ultérieure de liens explicites entre rêve et monde réel. (3) Par la fusion de mondes référentiels, l’analyste élargit la description du monde réel du patient avec des images reconnaissables du rêve: le monde du rêve et le monde réel sont ainsi momentanément fusionnés. Dans notre présentation, nous soulignons que selon nos enregistrements les interprétations de rêve n’impliquent pas particulièrement la révélation d’idées refoulées et inconscientes, mais plutôt un rappel au patient de choses déjà connues que le patient hésite cependant à penser ou à exprimer.

Dieser Beitrag untersucht mithilfe der Gesprächsanalyse von Audioaufnahmen psychoanalytischer Sitzungen die Traumdeutung als Gesprächspraxis. Wir richten den Blick auf die verschiedenen Wege, auf denen die “in der Realität” geltenden Bedeutungen von im Traum vorkommenden Objekten und Ereignissen gemeinschaftlich geschaffen werden. Es wurden drei verschiedene Wege zur Bedeutungsschaffung ermittelt. (1) In klaren Aussagen benennt entweder der Analytiker oder der Patient die Bedeutung eines Traumelements, beispielsweise mit der Feststellung, die Kuh im Traum stehe für Frauen. (2) Bei der Bedeutungsschaffung durch Neubeschreibung schildert der Analytiker aufs Neue Ereignisse aus der Realität oder dem Traum, die im vorangehenden Gespräch erwähnt wurden. Diese Neubeschreibung ermöglicht die anschließende Benennung expliziter Verknüpfungen zwischen dem Traum und der Realität. (3) Bei der Zusammenführung von Bezugswelten erweitert der Analytiker die Beschreibung der Realität durch den Patienten mit Bildern, die erkennbar aus dem Traum stammen: So werden Traumwelt und Realität vorübergehend zusammengeführt. In der Diskussion zeigen wir mit unseren Daten aus den Audioaufnahmen auf, dass es bei der Traumdeutung nicht in erster Linie darum geht, verdrängte und unbewusste Vorstellungen zutage zu fördern, sondern den Patienten an etwas zu erinnern was er bereits weiß, worüber er jedoch nur widerstrebend nachdenkt oder spricht.

Applicando il metodo dell’analisi della conversazione a una serie di sedute psicoanalitiche audioregistrate, il presente articolo esamina l’interpretazione dei sogni come pratica di conversazione. Ci concentreremo qui sui diversi modi in cui il significato sul piano del “mondo reale” di oggetti o eventi presenti nel sogno viene creato in collaborazione tra analista e paziente. Abbiamo individuato tre principali percorsi di creazione del significato. (1) Le affermazioni esplicite, in cui l’analista o il paziente afferma il significato di un elemento del sogno – stabilendo ad esempio che la vacca del sogno rappresenta le donne. (2) La creazione di significato attraverso una ridescrizione: un processo per cui l’analista (ri)racconta eventi appartenenti al mondo reale o al sogno ai quali si era già fatto riferimento in una fase precedente della conversazione. (3) Fondere insieme i due mondi di riferimento. Ciò accade quando l'analista estende la descrizione che il paziente fa del mondo reale con immagini che riconoscibilmente provengono dal sogno: il mondo del sogno e il mondo reale risultano in tal modo temporaneamente fusi l’uno con l’altro. Nella nostra discussione del materiale sottolineiamo peraltro come nelle registrazioni da noi utilizzate per questo studio l'interpretazione dei sogni non consiste prioritariamente nel rivelare idee rimosse e inconsce, bensì nel ricordare al paziente qualcosa che egli già conosce ma di cui è restio a parlare, o a cui non vuole pensare.

Mediante el análisis conversacional de sesiones psicoanalíticas grabadas en audio, el artículo investiga la interpretación de sueños como una práctica conversacional. Los autores se concentran en la manera en la que el significado de los objetos o los sucesos del “mundo real” en el sueño son creados colaborativamente. Se encontró tres caminos para la creación de significado. (1) En afirmaciones sencillas, el analista o el paciente afirma el significado de un elemento onírico, por ejemplo, estableciendo que la vaca en el sueño significa mujeres. (2) En la redescripción, el analista vuelve a describir sucesos que pertenecen al mundo real o al sueño, a los cuales se ha hecho referencia en la conversación anterior. Esta redescripción permite la subsiguiente afirmación de vínculos explícitos entre el sueño y el mundo real. (3) En la fusión de mundos referenciales, el analista extiende la descripción del mundo real del paciente con imágenes claramente provenientes del sueño: el mundo onírico y el mundo real son así fusionados por un momento. En la sección de debate, los autores ponen de relieve que en sus datos de grabaciones en audio, la interpretación de sueños no implica principalmente revelar las ideas reprimidas e inconscientes, sino más bien hacer recordar al paciente algo que ya sabe pero es reacio a pensar o a hablar de ello.

responseSet.authorMast: Nicolás Suárez Delucchi and  Pablo Fossa Arcila
responseSet.authorCitation: Suárez Delucchi, N. & Fossa Arcila, P.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vygotsky’s inner language and its relation to the unconscious system of Freud

Nicolás Suárez Delucchi and Pablo Fossa Arcila

Pablo Fossa Arcila

Macul
Santiago
Chile

Providencia
Santiago
Chile

Las Condes
Santiago
Chile

Pablo Fossa Arcila  

Inner language, unconscious representations, primary process, derivative/formation of the unconscious, Freud, Vygotsky

This article develops a theoretical articulation between Freud’s conceptions about the functioning of the unconscious system and the phenomenon described by Vygotsky as “inner language.” We describe Vygotsky’s theoretical work on inner language and relate it to Freudian postulates regarding the functioning of the unconscious system and the passage from representations of this system to consciousness. Although Freud and Vygotsky come from different traditions and different understandings of psychological functioning, we consider that a dialogue between the two positions and their theoretical proposals can contribute to a better understanding of the phenomenon of inner language in the human experience. Following Freud, we conclude that the origin of inner language meets the conditions for being considered a derivative or formation of the unconscious, given that its phenomenological description is consistent with the functioning of the primary process as well as with a compromise formation between desire and censorship.

Cet article développe une articulation théorique entre les conceptions de Freud sur le fonctionnement du système inconscient et le phénomène décrit par Vygotsky comme un «langage intérieur». Nous décrivons ces travaux théoriques de Vygotsky sur le langage intérieur pour les comparer aux postulats freudiens concernant le fonctionnement du système inconscient et le passage des représentations de ce système à la conscience. Bien que Freud et Vygotsky proviennent de traditions différentes et de différentes compréhensions du fonctionnement psychologique, nous considérons qu'un dialogue entre les deux positions et leurs propositions théoriques pourrait contribuer à une meilleure compréhension du phénomène du langage intérieur dans le vécu humain. En accord avec Freud, nous concluons que l'origine de tout langage intérieur répond aux conditions nécessaires pour être considéré comme un dérivé ou une formation de l'inconscient, étant donné que sa description phénoménologique est comparable au fonctionnement du processus primaire ainsi qu’aux formations de compromis entre désir et censure.

Dieser Beitrag entwickelt eine theoretische Verbindung zwischen Freuds Konzeptionen zur Funktionsweise des unbewussten Systems und dem Phänomen, das bei Wygotski als ,innere Sprache‘ bezeichnet wird. Wir beschreiben Wygotskis theoretische Arbeit zur inneren Sprache und stellen eine Verbindung zu freudianischen Postulaten hinsichtlich der Funktionsweise

des unbewussten Systems und des Übergangs von Repräsentationen dieses Systems in das Bewusstsein her. Freud und Wygotski gehören zwar unterschiedlichen Traditionen an und unterscheiden sich in ihrem Verständnis des psychologischen Wirkens, aber ein Dialog zwischen den beiden Positionen und den dazugehörigen theoretischen Angeboten kann zu einem besseren Verständnis des Phänomens der inneren Sprache im menschlichen Erleben führen. Freud folgend kommen wir zu dem Schluss, dass der Ursprung der inneren Sprache die Bedingungen dafür erfüllt, eine Ableitung oder Gebilde des Unbewussten zu sein, wenn man annimmt, dass ihre phänomenologische Beschreibung mit der Funktionsweise des Primärprozesses ebenso im Einklang ist wie mit einer Kompromissbildung zwischen Begehren und Zensur.

Il presente articolo mette in relazione in una prospettiva teorica le idee freudiane sul funzionamento del sistema inconscio e il fenomeno descritto da Vygotsky con il nome di “linguaggio interno.” Il lavoro teorico di Vygotsky sul linguaggio interno viene qui descritto e accostato ai postulati freudiani riguardanti il funzionamento del sistema inconscio e il passaggio dalle rappresentazioni di tale sistema alla conscienza. Sebbene Freud e Vygotsky facessero capo a diverse tradizioni e a diversi modi di intendere il funzionamento della psiche, a nostro parere un dialogo tra le due posizioni e le rispettive proposte teoriche può portare a una miglior comprensione del fenomeno del linguaggio interno e del suo ruolo nell’esperienza umana. Seguendo Freud, si perviene qui alla conclusione che l’origine del linguaggio interno soddisfa le condizioni perché questo sia considerato come derivato o formazione dell’inconscio, nella misura in cui dal punto di vista fenomenologico la sua descrizione appare coerente tanto con il funzionamento del processo primario quanto con una formazione di compromesso tra desiderio e censura.

El presente trabajo desarrolla una articulación teórica entre la concepción del funcionamiento del sistema inconsciente en Freud y el fenómeno del “lenguaje interno” de Vygotsky. Se describe el trabajo teórico de Vygotsky sobre el lenguaje interno y se lo relaciona con los postulados freudianos sobre el funcionamiento del sistema inconsciente y el pasaje de las representaciones de este sistema a la consciencia. Aunque Freud y Vygotsky provienen de diferentes tradiciones y distintas maneras de entender el funcionamiento psicológico, se considera que el diálogo entre las dos posiciones y sus propuestas teóricas puede contribuir a una mejor comprensión del fenómeno del lenguaje interno en la experiencia humana. Siguiendo a Freud, se concluye que el origen del lenguaje interno cumple las condiciones de los derivados o las formaciones del inconsciente, dado que su descripción fenomenológica es congruente con el funcionamiento del proceso primario y con una formación de compromiso entre el deseo y la censura.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The unobtrusive relational analyst

Explorations in psychoanalytic companioning, by Robert Grossmark, London & New York, Routledge, 2018, 207 pp., £32.99 (paperback), ISBN-978-1-138-89906-3

Riccardo Lombardi

Riccardo Lombardi

Roma
Italy

Riccardo Lombardi   dr.riccardolombardi@libero.it

From the exploration of so-called “difficult” patients Robert Grossmark has distilled a patient-centred psychoanalysis with new and innovative implications not only for a relational approach but also for all the diktats of the psychoanalytic training institutes, promoting a clear emancipation from the obsession with orthodoxy in favour of clinical evidence. As the author himself says,

The treatment belongs to the patient, and it is the analyst’s work to find and join with the register and wavelength that is the truest expression of the patient’s inner world and experience … The psychoanalyst can unobtrusively companion the patient into areas of non-developed, non-related, and non-represented inner life in the register that is organic to the patient at that time. (Ibid., 3–4, my italics)

He writes of exploring the most archaic areas of individual development which are often found in patients who are not clinically psychotic but have “defects of thinking” (Bion) such as we often find in the most seriously ill patients:

Often, psychoanalytic treatment eschewed patients who did not rely primary on language and consequently could not engage with both the structure and the spirit of the psychoanalytic endeavour, seeing them as too narcissistic, disorganized, or primitive. Action, motility, and pure sensation tended to be classed as acting out, resistances, or assault on the treatment. (…) However, many patients who present in this way also harbor self-states that contain earlier undeveloped, empty, unspeakable, and profoundly non-related parts of themselves that can find no expression in language. These areas of the self are much less likely to be reached by dialogical engagements and are often chased underground or shamed by a psychoanalytic treatment that puts a premium on relatedness, thought, and verbal exploration. (Grossmark, 2018, 1–3)

This centring on the patient which Grossmark introduces has the virtue of projecting psychoanalysis into the future, opening up the conventional boundaries of analysability and detaching psychoanalytic exploration from the dogma of transference interpretations and the relational religion. This quality of independence endows the present volume with special importance and places it on a level above the sectarian distinction between schools, making it an admirable example of psychoanalytic research unburdened by ideologies and essentially centred on respect for the clinical facts.

[[RunningHead]]

Mourir d’écrire ? Shoah, traumas extrêmes et psychanalyse des survivants

by R. Rosenblum, Paris, PUF, 2019, €23, 188 pp., [Death from writing? The holocaust, extreme traumas and the psychoanalysis of survivors], ISBN: 978-2-13-080100-9

Gilbert Diatkine

Gilbert Diatkine

Gilbert Diatkine   gilbert.diatkine@wanadoo.fr

Rachel Rosenblum is one of the rare French psychoanalysts who expresses herself as well in English as she does in French. The research that she has carried out over the last 20 years or so on the psychoanalysis of extreme traumas has been published in both English language and French language journals. They are gathered together and often expanded in her book Mourir d’écrire? Shoah, traumas extrêmes et psychanalyse des survivants.

This rich study had its origin in a trauma, the suicide in 1994 of a friend, philosopher and probable future psychoanalyst, Sarah Kofman. This suicide occurred on 5 October 1994, just after the publication of an autobiographical narrative, Rue Ordener, rue Labat (Ordener Street, Labat Street) (Kofman 1994) in which Sarah Kofman recounts, in direct style and in the first person, how, after being hidden by a neighbour at the moment when her father was taken from their home by the Nazis, she became attached to her to the point of repudiating her own mother. Did she commit suicide on account of what she had written? It is impossible to establish a certain causality between the publication of this book and her suicide, but Rachel Rosenblum was led to ask herself whether, far from having a cathartic effect as is often supposed, speaking or writing about an extreme trauma might itself result in death.

Can one die from writing? The question was all the more urgent in that, around the same time, several great writers who had survived the camps, or former hidden children who had survived the disappearance of their families, had committed suicide; these included Romain Gary, Piotr Rawicz, Primo Levi and Paul Célan. Other writers, like Robert Antelme, Michel del Castillo and Jorge Semprun, did not die after publishing their testimony about the camps, but told journalists that they felt writing was putting them at risk.

The question has considerable significance for psychoanalysts: do they risk involuntarily putting their patients at danger by asking them to “say everything” and by uncovering secrets whose revelation can have devastating effects? A case courageously reported by Sydney Stewart and studied by Rachel Rosenbaum shows how real this danger is: Karl survived an extermination camp as a young adolescent. He consulted Stewart in connection with anxiety attacks and minor sexual disorders. A Jew born in Czechoslovakia, Karl took American nationality after his liberation and married a German woman who, he said, did not know that he was Jewish. He had sadomasochistic sexual relations with her during which she mistreated him as a “little Jewish boy”. During the analysis, Karl revealed that he was allergic to fish. Around the fifth month of the analysis he dreamt that he was being attacked by a giant fish and woke up with swollen lips, as if he was suffering from an allergic reaction. The analyst asked him what the fish made him think of. The patient suddenly recalled, with intense anxiety, the carp that 1059

[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

August Baker

August Baker

Waban
MA

August Baker   ajbaker5@bu.edu

Dear Dr Birksted-Breen,

Lear (2019) invites readers to test his opinions in open debate and conversation (1105). In that spirit, I provide the comments below, organized in two sections based on Lear’s distinction between: (1) everyday life organized by Socratic dialogue, and (2) the psychoanalytic respite organized by the fundamental rule.

Everyday life and Socratic dialogue

For Lear, everyday life is organized by Socratic speech according to which we feel answerable to others. This is a problematic conception in several ways.

First, Lear says we feel an obligation to answer why questions: for example, why we have the beliefs that we do – and why we have the desires we do. I understand that we are sometimes expected to answer such questions. Applying for a job you might be asked why you want the position. A résumé says, “Seeking to leverage strong leadership skills and expertise to increase profitability.” But after the hermeneutics of suspicion (Ricoeur, 1970, 32–36), I think we pay lip service to these statements. In why questions, both the asker and answerer know this is at one remove.

Consider statements such as: “It’s not about the money”, “I’m not a competitive person” or “I didn’t intend to hurt your feelings.” Or at the first day, you go around the room, everyone saying why they are taking the class (what they hope to get out of it). I think people understand the authenticity quotient is low. In fact, one mental-health counselling approach considers it a problem if someone sincerely believes their answers why. Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT) explains to clients that these narratives they tell themselves – about what kind of person they are – are nonsensical:

From an early age, children are … are harassed into answering “why” questions. … In fact, children have little to say at first … “Why” questions are often met honestly with the answer “Just because.” … Eventually, however, children learn to tell coherent stories to explain their behavior that are acceptable to others. (Luoma, Hayes, and Walser, 2017, 21–22)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Estructuración psíquica y subjetivación del niño de escolaridad primaria. El trabajo de la latencia [Psychic structuration and subjectivization of the elementary school age child: The latency work]

by Rodolfo Urribarri, Buenos Aires, Colección Conjunciones, Ed. Novedades Educativas, 2008, 296 pp., $745 (argentinian pesos, paperback, Conjunciones directed by Marcela Pereira; Translations of quotes are by Julieta Paglini), ISBN 978-987-538-213-8

Julieta Paglini

Julieta Paglini

Córdoba
Argentina
Cordoba Psychoanalytic Society

Julieta Paglini   julietapaglini@hotmail.com

The author seeks to draw attention to the psychic structuring and subjectivization of elementary school children, which is made possible by latency work. He suggests that we cast aside our understanding of latency as a chronological period, during which all drive activity is suspended, that dull wait for the advent of puberty. He introduces us to the laborious “latency work,” which involves the subject in an intra- and intersubjective elaboration. Culture and its effects upon the structuring of the psychic apparatus are given particular attention.

Urribarri presents a psyche in perpetual movement and considers latency as one of the crucial moments where the resignification of lived experience is played out, as the production of a neogenesis.

The reader is given a meticulous tour of “the transformational metapsychological byways of the latency work” and their implications for the construction of subjectivity. The author reinforces the idea of “work,” endowed with an intense movement of psychic working through, both with regard to the object as well as to narcissism, rather than as a “period,” the latter more being more associated with the temporal rather than with psychic movements.

I highly recommend reading first the foreword by the pioneering Madeleine Baranger, who confers depth and gives pleasure to the act of reading.

I shall pause, therefore, to consider the term “work,” which in Freud finds its point of departure in “The Interpretation of Dreams” (1900), a text today considered foundational to psychoanalysis. It is here that he develops his concept in relation to psychic activity, the possible paths that find their beginnings in desire, and the dream work, which transforms latent thoughts into manifest content. Later on, Freud points more specifically to the “work of mourning,” with its cathexis and decathexis, in which the loss of the object gives rise to a symbolic work, that of representation. This unconscious task involves the creation of something new, as with elaboration.

responseSet.title: Dori Laub, MD (1937–2018)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dori Laub, MD (1937–2018)

Nanette Auerhahn

Nanette Auerhahn

Cleveland Psychoanalytic Center

Nanette Auerhahn   CONTACT Nanette Auerhahn nanetteca@aol.com

Yaffe Eliach (1982, 4) tells a story of concentration camp inmates made to jump a huge pit or be shot. Among them was the rabbi of Bluzhov, Rabbi Israel Spira, and his friend, a freethinking Polish man. After they both successfully jumped over the abyss, the Polish man asked, “Tell me, Rebbe, how did you do it?” The rabbi answered, “I was holding onto my ancestral merit. I was holding on to the coattails of my father and my grandfather and my great-grandfather, of blessed memory … Tell me, my friend, how did you reach the other side of the pit?” “I was holding on to you,” replied the rabbi’s friend.

When teetering on the brink of Nazi infamy, avoiding plunging into the pit requires “an extraordinary moral force” – to wit, a powerful relationship with either God or an extraordinary human being (Lengyel 1946). For Holocaust survivors, their families and international scholars, the moral force that has allowed them to mentalize the Holocaust has been Dori Laub. He assumed that role for me as well: for years, I have written about the Holocaust holding onto Dori.

In the film Son of Saul, a member of the Sonderkommando, Saul, becomes preoccupied with a boy who miraculously survives the gas chamber only to be murdered by a guard. Saul’s obsession with the boy is a kind of fetish that stands both for reality and in the place of reality, screening out one’s hopelessness and helplessness when the limits of human tolerance are exceeded. In the face of annihilation anxiety, Saul holds onto a fantasy of hope that the death machine is neither invincible nor inevitable, as signified by a boy who lived lest, when faced with the abyss, he go mad (Auerhahn and Laub 2019).

Dori recognized that the presence of another is critical in providing the discipline and self-restraint that allows one to stay present long enough to pursue and describe trauma without fleeing or turning the experience off. The dialogic structure of the self underlaid the centrality of empathy, testimony and witnessing to his understanding of massive psychic trauma. Dori authored over 30 articles and numerous book chapters on the representation of trauma, including Testimony: Crises of Witnessing in Literature, Psychoanalysis and History (1991) with Shoshana Felman, and Psychoanalysis and Holocaust Testimony: Unwanted Memories of Social Trauma (2017) with Andreas Hamburger. With Laurel Vlock, he co-founded the Fortunoff Yale Videoarchive for Holocaust Testimonies, which has recorded over 4500 testimonies and made possible educational materials about the Holocaust, the Cambodian genocide, the World War II internment of Japanese Americans, and the ethnic cleansing of the former Yugoslavia. Beginning in 1990, Dori co-chaired with Ira Brenner APsaA’s annual discussion group on The Effects of the Holocaust on Survivors and their Families.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vanda Shrenger Weiss. La prima psicoanalista in Italia. La psicoanalisi a Roma in epoca fascista

[Vanda Shrenger Weiss: The First Psychoanalyst in Italy. Psychoanalysis in Rome during the Fascist Era], by Rita Corsa, Rome, Alpes, 2017, 380 pp., 353 pp., €21.25, ISBN 9788865314159

Ambra Cusin

Ambra Cusin

Italy
S.P.I. Psychoanalyst

Ambra Cusin   ambra.cusin@spiweb.it

Rita Corsa’s book is a biography of Vanda Shrenger Weiss, the first psychoanalyst in Italy, of whom little has actually been written. In particular, the book explores the period in which Shrenger Weiss, the wife of Edoardo Weiss, the first Italian psychoanalyst, lived in Italy and collaborated in the foundation of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society (SPI) and in the birth of the Italian journal Rivista di Psicoanalisi. In addition, the period in which Shrenger Weiss abandoned Italy during the Fascist regime and emigrated with her husband to the United States is explored, as well as her move towards joining the Jungian Society. Translations or summaries of some of Shrenger Weiss’s most significant writings are also included in the book.

I am writing this book review on 27 January, International Holocaust Remembrance Day, a time when we remember not only the tragedy of Nazi persecutions of the Jews, but all the persecutions that, despite the many “never more” repetitions over the years, continue to strike innocent people in many parts of the world. And I am writing in Trieste, Italy, where 80 years ago Mussolini announced the shameful racial laws.

With this book—which makes use of original archival material, often unpublished, as the author herself affirms—Rita Corsa presents us with the product of having patiently sifted through various sources, in old offices where the smell of dust and worm-eaten pages pervades—a work of in-depth archival research and direct testimonials of the protagonists’ living descendants. The book legitimizes a significant figure in psychoanalysis, Vanda Shrenger Weiss, but it is also a cross-section of an era steeped in tragic events that, unfortunately, seem to echo in various forms in much of our everyday life.

*Translated by Gina Atkinson, M.A.427

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Parentalidades y género. Su incidencia en la subjetividad

[Parenting and gender: their impact on subjectivity], edited by Patricia Alkolombre and Cândida Sé Holovko, Buenos Aires, Letra Viva, 2016, 327 pp., 30€ (paperback), ISBN 9789506496609

Ester Palerm

Ester Palerm

Barcelona
Spain

Ester Palerm   palerm.mari@gmail.com

This book is the product of the work of a group of Latin American psychoanalysts who have studied the extent to which social transformations and the advance of reproductive technologies have favoured the emergence of new family configurations, and the ways in which such configurations affect subjectivity. All the articles were presented at the Latin American Dialogue XI organized by the IPA’s Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis (COWAP). The work is divided into eight broad sections, where readers will find significant contributions on the following topics: the maternal function; the various articulations among kinship, filiation and blood ties generated by the new modes of conception and gestation; childhood and adulthood identity issues tied to the presence of intersex physical features; perverted parenting; conflicts in the maternal function; and fragmentation of parental functions in new extended families. At the same time, the book is a tribute to Mariam Alizade, who was founding member of COWAP and inspired the Latin American Dialogue I (held in 1999), among many contributions. There is an article by Alizade in the first section of the book.

We are, thus, in the presence of a work that covers a broad range of topics with a pluralistic approach. The theoretical contributions, accompanied by ample clinical illustrations, foster reflection and, at the same time, challenge the current validity and limitations of some basic psychoanalytic concepts, or point to the need to expand or reformulate them. In this line, in the context of new family configurations, several authors address parenting in terms of the maternal and paternal functions, which may be performed by the biological mother or father, or by any other person.

Largely, the articles highlight the challenges these transformations pose to psychoanalysis. They argue that one of the risks we run is clinging to traditional psychoanalytic theory and pronouncing anything that does not fit into it as pathological so as to avoid the uncertainties of a shifting world. In addition, the authors suggest that in order to approach and understand the complexity of these phenomena (such as the new ways of becoming a parent, or gender variants and their effects on subjectivation), toward which analysts must show an open-minded attitude, we need to maintain our analytic listening so that we can face the clinical experience without being dominated by prejudice.

responseSet.title: When the sun bursts: The enigma of schizophrenia: by Christopher Bollas, Yale, University Books, 2015, 212 pp., $18 (hardback), ISBN: 9780300223651
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When the sun bursts: The enigma of schizophrenia

by Christopher Bollas, Yale, University Books, 2015, 212 pp., $18 (hardback), ISBN: 9780300223651

Kathy Taylor

Kathy Taylor

London
UK

Kathy Taylor   ktaylor_psy@yahoo.co.uk

When the Sun Bursts is a richly condensed book, written from Christopher Bollas’s extensive clinical experience of schizophrenia. With his characteristic pithy style, it is vividly alive with textured description and compassionate resonance of the experience of both living with schizophrenia and attempting to work with it clinically. Bollas wants us to know in this book how much he respects the schizophrenic patients he has worked with and to share with us just how difficult is the work of being in the experience and making sense of it, for both patient and analyst. In Part One, Bollas gives an illustrative account of his chronological journey in developing his experience with psychosis. He moves, in a somewhat autobiographical way, from his experience working with psychotic and autistic children at East Bay Activity Centre in California, USA, to beginning as a counsellor with students at the University of Buffalo and then to his move to London to train at the Tavistock Clinic and the Institute of Psychoanalysis. Theory is addressed in Part Two of the book – his way of thinking about why schizophrenic people behave in the ways they do. In Part Three, he turns to his views on the psychotherapy of schizophrenia in a brief final section.

Bollas describes his book as the record of his development in understanding psychosis: “to work with a schizophrenic was to study the enigma of being human and the potential to lose one’s mind” (2). He is clear that he is not intending to address causal explanations for schizophrenia and that he will not review the vast literature on causation, treatment or outcomes. The book also does not address differential diagnoses; it seems to include the complete range of people who have experienced psychotic experiences at some point, whether short or long term. The focus, however, is clearly on psychotic experience as clearly recognisable to a psychiatrist – involving a significant break with, or a distortion of, reality, and resulting in significant difficulties.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis: a general theory of psychical structure formation and pathogenesis: by Peter Zagermann, Queens, IP Books, 2019, 341 pp., $35, ISBN: 978-1949093353
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis: a general theory of psychical structure formation and pathogenesis

by Peter Zagermann, Queens, IP Books, 2019, 341 pp., $35, ISBN: 978-1949093353

Elliot Kronish

Elliot Kronish

Elliot Kronish   elkronish@aol.com

Dr Peter Zagermann is an IPA psychoanalyst practising in Munich, Germany, working with child, adolescent and adult patients. The book under review, Psychoanalysis: A General Theory of Psychical Structure Formation and Pathogenesis, contains no biographical information. But we do have comments of endorsement from prominent analytic thinkers such as Kernberg and Bolognini, both of whom highlight the general impressions I had in reading the book. The theses put forward here are complex, novel and intriguing. I was especially taken with the reformulations given to some of our most basic psychoanalytic concepts, particularly the origin and aims of the life and death drives, the essence of the castration threat, the ever-present dialectic of the ego-object situation, and the commentary, derived from these ideas, on different forms of psychopathology.

For Zagermann the pre-schizoid position is the earliest state of the psyche. In it the ego (in its earliest sensory/perceptual position) experiences the object as the “all-that-is,” as the totality of plenitude, as life itself. What is inevitably felt as lack (tension due to need) is dealt with not by splitting (good/bad object) but rather by a megalomaniacal type fusion with the “all-that-is” (life sustaining object); this is primary narcissism. In this phase there can be no “bad object” because of the “all-that-is” situation. The rudimentary ego cannot conceive of a “bad object” because its fusional mode of operation would then lack any source of life-giving sustenance. Representational object constancy, time, and comparison of different states of mind are not experienced, and therefore no object-ego splitting can occur as described by Klein in the paranoid-schizoid position.

To reach the paranoid-schizoid position, the primitive mind must be able to develop object constancy; that is, it begins to “remember” (i.e. represent) good experiences with the object. This in turn can lead to a developmentally appropriate (i.e. expected) form of splitting. At this point the good object can be remembered (i.e. sustained) to counteract the experience of the bad object (created through lack of satisfaction of need), and helps the ego survive the paranoid position of attacks by the bad object (reinforced by the aggressive projections of the ego). In turn, the experience of remembering these good/bad experiences leads to the development of a sense of time and continuity, essential to the development of a sense of reality, and ultimately to overcoming the object split (the working through of the anxieties inherent in the depressive position).

responseSet.title: Response to Jon Mills
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Jon Mills

Eyal Rozmarin

Eyal Rozmarin

USA

Eyal Rozmarin   eyal.rozmarin@gmail.com

Dear Editor,

The publication of a book is, as the term itself suggests, a public gesture. A book aims to reach and impact the minds and hearts of people, and the communities they inhabit. In the case of a psychoanalytic book, this gesture aims to operate in yet another register, to influence the way we think and work with people who put in us their deepest trust. Publishing an analytic book therefore carries with it a great deal of responsibility, to the readers and writers, clinicians and patients who make up this deep but also precarious field of theory and practice. And so, reviewing a book demands responsibility of a similar kind. Responsibility towards the book’s scholarly ambition and labor, but even more so towards the collective discourse in which it aims to take part. It is in this spirit that I made my best effort to review the book at hand.

Relational psychoanalysis is by now a wide framework of psychoanalytic inquiry. From object relations to field theory, from attachment theory to deconstruction, from a focus on the nuclear family to questioning the place of the social and political at the core of our intimate lives. It spans the range from the conservative to the radical, from the daring to the quotidian. It is a sprawling and uneven theoretical landscape. And like any emerging scholarly field making a claim for its perspectives on knowledge, relational psychoanalysis deserves rigorous scrutiny. As I have written in my review, it is not the book’s critical ambition, but the rigor of its critique that I found problematic.

Mills argues in his letter to the editors that my review of his book is intellectually dishonest, that it is politically motivated, and that I am gaslighting the reader, in these words. This response sadly persists in the manner that constitutes one of the book’s most prominent shortcomings: conflating scholarship with explicitly personalized interpretations and accusations, such that the reader is rendered disoriented and ill served. There is in Mills’ letter a parallel conflation between scholarly affiliation and partizanship. One being related to, and more familiar, with a particular scholarly perspective, does not necessarily mean conflict with others, or an inability to think about one’s own perspective critically. It might actually, and hopefully, mean that one can evaluate better ideas in and about that field.

responseSet.title: The psychoanalytic reason, an Oedipal reason: by Jaime I Szpilka, Madrid, Editorial Mentecata Ediciones, 2014, 238 pp., 18.00€, ISBN 978-84-941144-2-7
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The psychoanalytic reason, an Oedipal reason

by Jaime I Szpilka, Madrid, Editorial Mentecata Ediciones, 2014, 238 pp., 18.00€, ISBN 978-84-941144-2-7

Leonardo Peskin

Leonardo Peskin

Ciudad Autonoma de Buenos Aires
Argentina

Leonardo Peskin   leonardopeskin@hotmail.com

A brief review of this book is no easy task given the conceptual density that characterizes Szpilka’s body of work. I was recently given the opportunity to introduce and discuss several of his works, in addition to reviewing his biography and collected works. This, alongside the long history of our relationship, places me in a position to make some broad stroke observations in regard to locating this text within his wider thought.

Shortly after writing a beautiful and profound collection of short stories, La Tzibeles (2013), which reveals several autobiographical details, Szpilka wished to return to an earlier work (Believing in the Unconscious).

Perhaps the book of short stories may be considered an attempt to explore the fantasmatic dimensions of his own identity and, by way of a self-analysis, to find another way to explore certain original themes in greater depth; original in the dual sense of the origin of human reason, and the origin of himself as a person. La Tzibeles reflects a sophisticated humour and a subtle characterization of the vicissitudes of the life of the protagonist, seldom found in literature written by psychoanalysts. In fact, this is a portrayal of the innermost workings of the author’s own personality. In essence the stories boil down to this: the things that are seemingly most consistent in a person’s life turn out to reveal their inconsistency in manifold, humorous and ingenious ways, leading us to the conclusion that one should in fact “believe in the unconscious”. This is a theme that has clearly been developed over various books and articles, but it is here, in this book, which forms the subject of my review, that it is explored in the greatest depth.

If we consider these other works as a counterpart to the book of short stories and his present work, the vivid nature of the inexistence of an identity – which Szpilka describes in La Tzibeles as an “ornament of nothingness”– is arguably revisited in this text with greater erudition and depth with regard to what he meant by “nothingness”.

responseSet.title: The analyst’s reveries: by Fred Busch, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 106 p., £34,99 (paperback), ISBN-978-0-367-13417-4
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The analyst’s reveries

by Fred Busch, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 106 p., £34,99 (paperback), ISBN-978-0-367-13417-4

Fulvio Mazzacane

Fulvio Mazzacane

Pavia
Italy

Fulvio Mazzacane   fulvio.mazzacane@gmail.com

Busch’s interest in the topic of reverie springs from listening to a clinical vignette in which Claudio Eizirik shares a reverie he had while with his patient. Initially, Busch is puzzled; he experiences the sense of loss one feels when stepping out of the familiar schemes on which one has built one’s analytic style. Busch then surrenders to the experience, taking in his colleagues’ descriptions of their work with clinical reveries and ultimately generating a book that stimulates new ideas for the author while challenging already accepted ideas. The book offers a useful comparison between the various psychoanalytic schools and brings out the difficulties of entering into a clinical-theoretical scheme other than the one we are most familiar with. It is not easy to question some automatisms that we carry with us, to plunge into another psychoanalytic system, to study how it works and discuss it.

The aim should not be an attempt at simultaneous translation, which might involve the risk of losing the innovative potential of ideas that are framed in a different perspective. The search for differences and points of contact, the effort of mutual comprehension, constitute a bridge and are useful in the creation of a common ground.

Quite correctly, Busch points out that Bion hardly talks about reverie except in the mother–child relationship. It is post-Bionian developments that place reverie at the centre of clinical psychoanalysis, as one of the most important tools at the analyst’s disposal.

Busch himself refers to the way in which Ogden works on Susan Isaacs’s article (1943); he quotes:

The important thing is what one is able to do with the ideas Isaacs makes explicit in combination with the ideas that her language suggests … In addition, and probably more important, I have in mind of my own, and that allows me to see in her work a good deal that she did not see. The same is true for you the reader, in reading Isaacs and in reading what I write . (Ogden 2011, 4)

Ogden allows for an arbitrary, expansive, with-no-philological-claims use of the Isaac’s thought. I agree that in tackling an article it is useful to exploit it thoroughly in order to transcend the intentio autoris, allowing the intentio operis to play out unrestrained, conjuring up the unfolding of the yet-unconceived themes suggested by the author at the time of writing. Bion specifically asks us to do that with his works, and that is what post-Bionian analysts have done with Bion’s texts and, notably, with the concept of reverie.

responseSet.title: Sabina Spielrein’s theory of the origin and development of language
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sabina Spielrein’s theory of the origin and development of language

Fatima Caropreso

Fatima Caropreso

Juiz de Fora
Brazil

Fatima Caropreso   CONTACT Fatima Caropreso fatimacaropreso@uol.com.br Federal University of Juiz de Fora, Rua José Lourenço Kelmer, s/n, Bairro São Pedro, CEP: 36036-900, Juiz de Fora, MG, Brazil
Psychoanalytic theory, Sabina Spielrein, language, thought, subconsciousness

In recent years, a growing acknowledgement of the importance and originality of Sabina Spielrein’s theoretical and clinical work has taken place. However, few studies have been specifically devoted to a thorough analysis of her theory, so a satisfactory understanding of her thinking has not yet been fully achieved. The objective of this article is to contribute to the understanding of Spielrein’s theory of language, based on the analysis of her two main papers on the subject, “The Origin of the Child’s Words ‘Papa’ and ‘Mama’” (1922) and “Some Analogies between the Thought of Children, Aphasia, and the Subconscious Thought” (1923). Reference is also made to some hypotheses formulated in her first works in order to clarify the assumptions of the theory of language she advances in the early 1920s. It is argued that Spielrein developed an original psychoanalytic perspective on language. She brought about an innovative contribution to the understanding of the origins and functioning of both language and thought, articulating linguistic and neurological knowledge with psychoanalytic hypotheses and her own observations.

Une reconnaissance récente et croissante de l’importance et de l’originalité des travaux théoriques et cliniques de Sabina Spielrein a eu lieu. Cependant, peu d’études ont été consacrées spécifiquement à une analyse approfondie de sa théorie, de sorte qu’une compréhension satisfaisante de sa pensée n’a pas encore été pleinement atteinte. L’objectif de cet article est de contribuer à la compréhension de sa théorie du langage sur la base de l’analyse de ses deux articles principaux à ce sujet: « L’origine des mots de l’enfant ‘Papa’ et ‘Maman’» (1922) et « Quelques analogies entre la pensée de l’enfant, celle de l’aphasique et la pensée subconsciente » (1923). Afin de clarifier ses conjectures avancées au début des années 1920 sur la théorie du langage, référence est faite aussi à celles-ci, datant de ses premiers travaux. L’article soutient que Spielrein a développé une perspective psychanalytique originale sur le langage en y apportant une contribution innovatrice à la compréhension des origines et du fonctionnement du langage et de la pensée, par l’articulation de ses connaissances linguistiques et neurologiques avec des hypothèses psychanalytiques et ses propres observations.

In den letzten Jahren hat die Bedeutung und Originalität von Sabina Spielreins theoretischer und klinischer Arbeit zunehmend Anerkennung gefunden. Es gibt jedoch bisher nur wenige Untersuchungen, die sich ausdrücklich einer gründlichen Analyse ihrer Theorie widmen, so dass ein zufriedenstellendes Verstehen ihres Denkens bisher nicht vollständig erreicht werden konnte. Ziel dieses Beitrags ist es, das Verstehen von Spielreins Theorie der Sprache durch eine Analyse ihrer beiden wichtigsten Schriften zum Thema zu erweitern: “Die Entstehung der kindlichen Worte ‘Papa’ und ‘Mama’” (1922) und “Einige Analogien zwischen dem Denken von Kindern, der Aphasie und dem unbewussten Denken” (1923). Der Beitrag bezieht sich außerdem auf einige Hypothesen in ihren ersten Werken, um die Annahmen ihrer Sprachtheorie näher zu erläutern, die sie in den frühen 1920er Jahren vorstellt. Der Beitrag argumentiert, dass Spielrein eine originale psychoanalytische Perspektive auf die Sprache entwickelte. Sie leistete einen innovativen Beitrag zum Verstehen der Entstehung und der Funktionsweise von Sprache und Denken und formulierte linguistisches und neurologisches Wissen anhand psychoanalytischer Hypothesen und eigener Beobachtungen.

Negli ultimi anni l’importanza e l’originalità del lavoro teorico e clinico di Sabina Spielrein sono state sempre più riconosciute. Resta tuttavia scarso il numero di studi specificamente dedicati ad analizzare nel dettaglio la sua teoria, per cui si può ben affermare che la nostra comprensione del suo pensiero non ha ancora raggiunto un livello soddisfacente. L’obiettivo del presente articolo è di contribuire alla comprensione della teoria del linguaggio della Spielrein sulla base di una lettura analitica dei suoi due principali lavori sull’argomento: “L’origine delle parole infantili ‘papà’ e ‘mamma’” (1922) e “Alcune analogie tra il pensiero del bambino, quello dell’afasico e il pensiero inconscio” (1923). Si farà comunque riferimento anche ad alcune ipotesi da lei formulate nei suoi primi scritti, in modo da chiarire i presupposti della teoria del linguaggio da lei successivamente sviluppata nei primi Anni ‘20. La conclusione qui proposta è che la Spielrein abbia sviluppato una prospettiva psicoanalitica originale sul linguaggio, offrendo un contributo innovativo per la comprensione delle origini e del funzionamento del linguaggio e del pensiero e raccordando anche le competenze che possedeva in campo linguistico e neurologico con ipotesi psicoanalitiche e con le sue stesse osservazioni.

En los últimos años hay un mayor reconocimiento de la importancia y originalidad del trabajo teórico y clínico de Sabina Spielrein. Sin embargo, hay pocos estudios dedicados específicamente a un análisis riguroso de su teoría, de manera que aún no se ha logrado una comprensión satisfactoria de su pensamiento. El objeto de este artículo es contribuir a la comprensión de la teoría del lenguaje de Spielrein, a partir del análisis de sus dos principales artículos sobre el tema: “El origen de las palabras infantiles ‘papá’ y ‘mamá’” (1922) y “Algunas analogías entre el pensamiento del niño, el del afásico y el pensamiento inconsciente” (1923). A fin de aclarar los supuestos de la teoría del lenguaje que propuso a principios de la década de 1920, también se hace referencia a algunas hipótesis formuladas en sus primeros trabajos. Se sostiene que Spielrein desarrolló una perspectiva psicoanalítica original sobre el lenguaje. Asimismo, produjo un aporte innovador a la comprensión de los orígenes del lenguaje y del funcionamiento del pensamiento, articulando un conocimiento lingüístico y neurológico con hipótesis psicoanalíticas y con sus propias observaciones.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bipolarity of the scenic and lexical modes of analytic listening: Commentary on Gabriel Sapisochin’s paper “Enactment: Listening to Psychic Gestures”**

Ralf Zwiebel

Ralf Zwiebel

Germany

Ralf Zwiebel   CONTACT Ralf Zwiebel rzwiebel@web.de

A brief preliminary note

Is it true that an ever-widening gulf exists in contemporary psychoanalysis between an as it were increasingly elaborated, publicized version of our discipline and its actual practice hour by hour and day by day? Might there perhaps also be a conflictual tension between an ideal type and a real type of practice? After all, whereas theoretical models of psychoanalytic treatment are described in more and more diverse and differentiated terms, the day to day practice of the “good enough” psychoanalyst is as a rule less spectacular and tends to bear a powerfully intuitive stamp. Nowadays every psychoanalyst is commonly said to have his1 individualized working model. With each new publication—if at all noticed among the constantly expanding plethora of literature—the individual psychoanalyst is called upon, and sometimes also motivated, to undertake a critical reflection on his personal working model. Given a thorough reading, it seems to me that two aspects can be distinguished here. The first is that the reader will have to translate the author’s vocabulary into his own terms in order to be able to reconstruct the author’s argument. In addition to this fundamental work of translation, a second step must be transposition into one’s personal thinking and practice. Taken together, these two steps permit a process of appropriation (if the text contains innovative aspects) or critical engagement (if the author’s argument is unconvincing). This is the task that confronts me too as a commentator on the present paper by Gabriel Sapisochin, in which a fundamental problem of treatment theory is discussed in detail and which also introduces the new concept of the psychic gesture. The task is to reconstruct the author’s working model and to subject it to a critical review from my own perspective and experience—that is, in terms of my personal working model. This emphasis on the innovative element appears justified given that the author after all invokes a “new conception of the registration of early interaction with the primary objects as sequences of images of psychic gestures exchanged by the subject with the other.” Having regard to the wide-ranging considerations at issue, which must also take account of earlier publications by Dr Sapisochin, I shall necessarily confine myself to a small number of aspects.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working in the dark: understanding the pre-suicide state of mind

by Don Campbell and Rob Hale, Abingdon, Routledge, 2017, 120 pp., £93.99 (hardback), ISBN: 978-0-415-6452-3; £23.99 (paperback), ISBN: 978-0-415-6453-0; £21.59 (ebook), ISBN: 978-1-315-73149-0

John Keene

John Keene

John Keene   john.keene@ntlworld.com

Don Campbell and Rob Hale take us movingly and practically through the analyst's nightmare territory of patients who may, and sometimes do kill themselves. They explore the ways in which skilful therapeutic work can help to avoid such events. This concise volume should be on the shelf of all analysts and mental health practitioners, who at some time in their career will be faced with what the authors call “the pre-suicide state.” Their detailed exploration of the complex dynamics involved in the run-up to a suicide attempt offers understanding and hope with which to engage in situations where both are under attack. With its warmth, realism, and presentation of analytic ideas as extensions of ordinary thinking about ourselves—rather than as a separate belief system—it elegantly fulfils its intention to add to the resources of individuals and teams working with suicides. Its wide-ranging attention to the psychoanalytic and wider literature is a useful antidote to any tendency to an oversimplified understanding of suicide.

The book opens with each author outlining his own professional journey into this painful world. The focus on the feelings that these clinical encounters evoke in the analysts and fellow professionals is a constant resource throughout the book. The authors have worked in the field for over 40 years, both of them for many years at the Portman Clinic, a National Health Service forensic clinic caring for people who are sexually deviant or violent. This long encounter with people who use attacks on their own or others’ bodies is built on earlier experiences: Don Campbell at the Hampstead Clinic (now the Anna Freud Centre) and the Brent Centre for Adolescents, and Rob Hale at St Mary's Teaching Hospital and the Tavistock Clinic. Their immensely humane approach starts from the impact that threats of suicide and suicidal acts have on those around the suicidal person, and the book opens with the authors’ personal stories as to how they became involved in this difficult area of clinical practice. For the overall picture, I cannot do better than quote the authors’ closing summary, which shows clearly the writers’ emotional involvement and their stance in relation to their work with suicidal patients.

What have our suicidal patients taught us about the pre-suicidal state of mind? First, we learned about the importance of listening to the patient, to their histories. The pre-suicide state of mind has a history. If we ignored our suicidal patients’ histories or colluded with them in ignoring their history, our patients were likely to move from telling us about themselves to showing us by enacting a suicide fantasy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference and alive moments

by R. D. Hinshelwood, London, Process Press, 2016, 239 pp., £19-95 (paperback), ISBN 978-1-899209-17-0

Karen Stobart

Karen Stobart

London NW3 5BY
UK
The Society of Analytical Psychology

Karen Stobart   karenstobart@btinternet.com

Professor Hinshelwood writes that he encountered psychoanalysis not long after Paula Heimann’s seminal papers of 1950 and 1960 (xii) when the concept of countertransference came to be seen as something that might be helpful to the analysis and not simply a distraction or confusion. Thus he can consider himself as having been part of the “rehabilitation” of the concept almost since its beginning. A sentence in the Preface describes how his recognition of the usefulness of countertransference as a clinical tool parallels that of his profession. “It has taken me until I have reached touching distance from the end of my career to be able to systematize my thoughts and to write this account” (xii). In reading this book, the reviewer gained the impression that Hinshelwood is describing his own quite original attempt among British analysts to reach a view of countertransference which synthesizes an object relations perspective, which tends to view it more as the result of the patient’s projection, and the relational one, which inclines rather towards seeing the subjective contribution of the analyst.

“Countertransference and Alive Moments. Help or Hindrance” could also be read as an account of how countertransference has influenced the development of the psychoanalytic identity: “The analyst’s understanding of the material is indelibly shaped by an understanding of himself” (160). Hinshelwood continues: “The person of the analyst, and his anxieties, is the point. The notion of countertransference embodies the reality that the ego of the analyst struggles too” (xiii). He defines countertransference as an “attention to process” (135) and sensitively conveys his understanding of the analyst’s sense of exposure whilst involved in the process. He illustrates this through his own clinical material and that of others. From 221

responseSet.title: On revoking the subject of work: Psychoanalytic interpretations of the American television series The Office
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On revoking the subject of work: Psychoanalytic interpretations of the American television series The Office

Gianluca Crepaldi

Gianluca Crepaldi

Austria

Gianluca Crepaldi   CONTACT Gianluca Crepaldi gianluca.crepaldi@uibk.ac.at Institute of Psychosocial Intervention, University of Innsbruck, Schoepfstrasse 3, A-6020 Innsbruck, Austria

The present paper seeks to use the conceptual framework of psychoanalysis to reconstruct the unconscious fantasies and latent meanings of a very popular and massively successful comedy series. The Office (2005–2013) is an American adaptation of a much shorter British production with the same title, which was originally broadcast in the early 2000s. The pivotal unconscious idea behind the script is thoroughly anarchistic in nature, because it implies the radical revocation of the working subject by means of humour. To argue this thesis, an examination of the setting, characters and narratives of the show is carried out against a psychoanalytic background. A whole series about a workplace is nothing new, but this is a staging of absurd situations where most of the time no work is done at all. The characters undergo a constant process of de-identification with work, which subsequently leads us towards a discussion of the psychodynamic aspects of postmodern work in general. Interdisciplinary references to literature, philosophy and critical theory complement and expand the psychoanalytic perspective on the TV series as a contemporary cultural objectivation.

responseSet.title: Response to Letter to the Editor of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Letter to the Editor of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis

Jonathan Lear

Jonathan Lear

Jonathan Lear   jlear@uchicago.edu

I thank August Baker for his thoughtful letter but I think it fails to understand my essay in two important dimensions, the meaning of rationality and the significance of the Sabbath analogy.

First, when I introduced the conception of humans as rational animals I did so in terms of Aristotle’s conception of beings who are capable of logos. This is significantly different from contemporary conceptions of rationality, which seem to me constricted, distorted and in need of the kind of critique that an historical perspective on the concept can offer. So, when Baker says, “Bion (1970) is precisely opposite Lear: reason is emotion’s slave”, this is not true. Plato and Aristotle could and did believe both that humans are “rational animals” and that for the most part, when it comes to humans, reason is emotion’s slave. By way of psychoanalytic comparison, Freud can say both “where id was ego shall become” and that for the most part “the ego is the servant of the id”. These are not opposites. In the first statement, Freud is talking about what he takes to be the direction of movement toward psychic heath, and in the second he is talking about how humans for the most part are.

Second, Baker says that I say, “Freud’s fundamental rule says ‘relax’ to the analysand.” But I do not say that. What I say is: “Basically, the fundamental rule is formed by saying to logos: relax!” The issue is relaxing the constraints of logos – not of asking the analysand to relax. The fundamental rule says nothing about what an analysand’s emotional state is supposed to be. Nor do I. In my experience, it is not at all easy for analysands to relax the constraints of logos. I make clear in my essay that the Sabbath analogy is complex. On the Sabbath we are not simply asked to relax, we are enjoined to take a rest from the work typical of the other days of the week. We are at the same time commanded to remember the Sabbath and make it holy. In other words, when it comes to the Sabbath, there is a lot we ought to do, and it is not simply a matter of “relaxing”. The secular analogy is that the fundamental rule is a rule – one we are supposed to follow and one which we, in analysis, usually do not. This is not about analysands relaxing, it is about their being enjoined to relax the everyday constraints of ordinary discourse. This is a big difference.

[[RunningHead]]

Tribute to Alain de Mijolla (1933–2019)

Denys Ribas

Denys Ribas

Denys Ribas   CONTACTDenys Ribas denysribas@free.fr

Alain de Mijolla died on 24 January 2019. Born on 15 May 1933, he lived to be 85 years old. At his funeral, his family and friends paid him affectionate tribute and his grandchildren spoke fondly of their Papé, as grandfathers are known in Provence, and as he called his own grandfather who with his grandmother had brought him up.

Captain Rimbaud’s desertion (1975b)

I will first give an example of the psychoanalyst Mijolla's work with his fine article of applied psychoanalysis about Arthur Rimbaud, which along with his other article on identification fantasies—Fantasmes d’identification: Jakob, Freud et Goethe (1975a)—was awarded the Maurice Bouvet prize in 1976. Work on the identifications that will give a book: Visions of the Ego (1981).

Modestly leaving to the numerous commentators on the poet's life and works the ambition of explaining the source of its creativity, Mijolla illustrates his theories concerning the importance and the forms of identifications by focusing specifically on what is missing from the works and the documents, namely the poet's father, Captain Frédéric Rimbaud.1 A young army volunteer, through his serious approach he became a non-commissioned officer then an officer promoted from the ranks. Sent to Algeria for eight years, after three years of combat he became head of the Arab office in Sebdou, a quieter post where his task was to administer matters relating to the native population and keep the army staff informed about their docility or any rumours circulating about the elusive Emir Abd-el Kader and his rebel troops. He returned to France in 1850 and married Vitalie Cuif in 1853 following his own father's death. Always posted far away from his family, he was never accompanied by them in his appointments, and he gave them a child on each of his return visits. He disappeared permanently from his family's life when Arthur was six years old, and this is the desertion to which Mijolla refers since Captain Rimbaud was never an army deserter, leaving only when he retired. Mijolla pertinently indicates that this time spent in his son's childhood and these procreations were enough to make impressions in a child's life. On condition of having the right. The article's title might have been the deprivation of this father by a mother who erased every trace of him from the 188

responseSet.title: The hands of gravity and chance: by Thomas H. Ogden, London, Sphinx Books, 2018, 300 pp., $16.95 (paperback), ISBN: 9781782203575
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The hands of gravity and chance

by Thomas H. Ogden, London, Sphinx Books, 2018, 300 pp., $16.95 (paperback), ISBN: 9781782203575

Sara Boffito

Sara Boffito

Milano
Italy

Sara Boffito   sboffito@hotmail.it

The Hands of Gravity and Chance is a family novel; everything happens between the members of a family and the existential tension of the characters follows the complex events resulting from their ties. Following the vicissitudes of those ties keeps the reader engaged in a narrative coloured with suspense, deep empathy and the painful weight of witnessing.

The book opens with a tragic and apparently punctual and fortuitous event. It is an event of chance. A girl falls down the stairs, and goes into a coma. Her mother is at her bedside and her two older brothers stay at home with their maternal aunt. We soon realize that the father died when the girl – Catherine – was a baby. As in a play of the Comedy of Art, the five characters who appear in the first, dramatic scene – Rose (the mother), Margaret (the aunt) and Rose’s children Erin (the elder), Damien (the second) and Catherine (the youngest) – never leave the stage. The initial event brings us into the folds of those complex ties that have led and will lead the members of this family to cross the boundaries of the unthinkable, to perform actions usually inconceivable.

The reader is guided in these areas, as if immersed in the mental flow of the characters. Dignity is given to every breath, every jolt, every nuance of thought through a style that reflects a specific aspect of the psychoanalyst's ear (Ogden and Ogden 2013), of his compassion. The author never judges, but remains open to follow how events, internal and external, could have led to such extreme choices and actions. Can a mother give up a child, choosing that it is no longer her own? Can she be a mother to some of her children and not to others? Can a mother be replaced? And what about a father? Can a brother and sister love each other as lovers with a love that does not, however, smack of perversion? And can they decide to raise a child together, as husband and wife? Can one decide to disappear from someone's life and still say one loves that person?

Thomas Ogden relates the internal and external movements of his characters with passion and compassion. He unfolds for the reader the destinies of their lives and the “gravity” of those lives. In the word “gravity”, which appears in the title, we find a nodal aspect of this story. Gravity is the force that causes Catherine to fall down the stairs, and it is also the “weight” of internal events that, like an avalanche, seem capable of triggering consequences “downstream” that would seem incomprehensible, but which are actually the inevitable consequence of a state of affairs unleashed “upstream”. Thus we find in Ogden's fiction a connection to the truths one finds in the consulting room. His deep psychoanalytic sensitivity finds its way into the novel, reflecting how patients turn to us to communicate something that concerns them but that does not seem possible to their conscience to come to terms with – whether it is the result of a disaster or the desire to live something that could not be either thought or lived. Their hope is to find an ear that will understand what does not seem conceivable but is actually the result of an ineluctable sequence of events in the inner world.
